screen.name: screen1
screen.instruct:
These containers are not just simple cans. There is an activated charcoal strip inside each one.
Click on the container with the puddle sample to see a close-up.
screen.caption: Control sample
Puddle sample
Activated charcoal strip
Sample of burned flooring

screen.name: screen2
screen.instruct:
As a standard, we'll use 3-phenyltoluene. If 3-phenyltoluene is detected later in our analysis, we'll know that the recovery worked.
Use the pipette to add a small amount of 3-phenyltoluene to the puddle sample.
screen.caption: Control sample
Puddle sample
3-phenyl-toluene

screen.name: screen3
screen.instruct: Great. Now use the pipette with a new tip to add the same amount of 3-phenyltoluene to the control sample.
screen.caption: Control sample
Puddle sample
3-phenyl-toluene

screen.name: screen4
screen.instruct:
The next step is to heat the samples.
Put the containers into the oven.
screen.caption: Control sample
Puddle sample

screen.name: screen5
screen.instruct:
We’ll have to heat the sample at 80 °C (176 °F) for 16 hours.
Close the oven and switch it on to start the process.
screen.caption: Control sample
Puddle sample

screen.name: screen6
screen.instruct:
Upon heating, chemicals in the flooring material will vaporize into the container’s air space.
The activated charcoal strip will absorb any vapors in the air space.
screen.caption: Activated charcoal strip
Sample of burned flooring

screen.name: screen8
screen.instruct:
The next step is to recover the vapors absorbed by the activated charcoal strips.
Use the forceps to transfer the little black strip from the LID of the can to the glass vial labeled "puddle".
screen.caption: puddle
control
Control sample
Puddle sample

screen.name: screen9
screen.instruct: Good. Next, transfer the little black charcoal strip with the forceps from the LID of the can to the glass vial labeled “control”.
screen.caption: puddle
control
Control sample
Puddle sample

screen.name: screen10
screen.instruct: Now use the pipette to transfer a certain amount of diethyl ether to both vials.
screen.caption: puddle
control
Diethyl ether

screen.name: screen11
screen.instruct:
The diethyl ether will dissolve any ignitable liquid residues that have been absorbed by the activated charcoal strips.
Close the vials and shake both of them gently.
screen.caption: puddle
control

screen.name: screen12
screen.instruct:
We’ll keep the charcoal strips in case we have to run the analysis again.
Use the forceps to take out each of the charcoal strips and place them into corresponding empty vials.
screen.caption: puddle
control

screen.name: screen13
screen.instruct:
As a second standard, we’ll use perchloroethylene. If we detect perchloroethylene later in our analysis, we’ll know that the testing is accurate.
Add a small amount of perchloroethylene to the puddle sample.
screen.caption: puddle
control
Perchloro-ethylene

screen.name: screen14
screen.instruct: Next, add the same amount of perchloroethylene to the control sample.
screen.caption: puddle
control
Perchloro-ethylene

screen.name: screen15
screen.instruct: Place both vials in the gas chromatograph.
screen.caption: puddle
control

screen.name: screen16
screen.instruct:
The gas chromatograph separates the chemicals in the sample.
This can take up to 20 minutes. Let me explain the process while we’re waiting.

screen.name: screen17
screen.instruct: When the sample is placed into the injector, the chemicals in the sample are transported through a large, thin column.
screen.caption: Injector
Detector
Column

screen.name: screen18
screen.instruct: Based on their different properties, the chemicals move through the column at different speeds.

screen.name: screen19
screen.instruct: Each chemical is detected as it comes out of the column and plotted into a graph.
screen.caption: Time (minutes)
Detector

screen.name: screen20
screen.instruct:
This is how the resulting graph looks.
Click on the "Gas chromatogram" and "Peak" to learn more about the graph.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram
Peak
Time (minutes)
screen.popup: The gas chromatogram is the graph that shows all of the chemicals in a sample plotted against the time they came out of the column.
A peak represents one particular chemical in a sample. The peak's height depends on the abundance of the chemical in the sample.

screen.name: screen21
screen.instruct:
Some substances, such as gasoline and other fuels, are a mixture of different chemicals.
The resulting gas chromatogram is a pattern with several peaks.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram of gasoline
Time (minutes)

screen.name: screen22
screen.instruct: The results are ready. Click on the monitor to analyze the data.

screen.name: screen23
screen.instruct: First, we’ll identify the patterns in the gas chromatogram of the puddle sample.
screen.text: Click on the first pattern in the gas chromatogram of the puddle sample.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)

screen.name: screen24
screen.instruct:
Use the green arrows to tab through the database.
Click MATCH if you found a match to the highlighted pattern in the puddle sample.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)

screen.name: screen25
screen.instruct: Click on the second pattern in the gas chromatogram of the puddle sample.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)
Perchloroethylene

screen.name: screen26
screen.instruct:
Use the green arrows to tab through the database.
Click MATCH if you found a match to the highlighted pattern in the puddle sample.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)
Perchloroethylene

screen.name: screen27
screen.instruct: Click on the third pattern in the gas chromatogram of the puddle sample.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)
Perchloroethylene
Gasoline

screen.name: screen28
screen.instruct:
Use the green arrows to tab through the database.
Click MATCH if you found a match to the highlighted pattern in the puddle sample.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)
Perchloroethylene
Gasoline

screen.name: screen29
screen.instruct: Click on the fourth pattern in the gas chromatogram of the puddle sample.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)
Perchloroethylene
Gasoline
Oak flooring

screen.name: screen30
screen.instruct:
Use the green arrows to tab through the database.
Click MATCH if you found a match to the highlighted pattern in the puddle sample.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)
Perchloroethylene
Gasoline
Oak flooring

screen.name: screen31
screen.instruct:
The last step of our analysis is to compare the puddle sample to the control sample.
Click “Add control sample” to start the comparison.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)
Perchloroethylene
Gasoline
Oak flooring
3-phenyltoluene

screen.name: screen32
screen.instruct: Are the patterns in the two gas chromatograms the same or different?
screen.wrongText:
Are you sure? The patterns look different to me.
Look at the different peaks and try again.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)
Perchloroethylene
Gasoline
Oak flooring
3-phenyltoluene
Gas chromatogram #2: control sample

screen.name: screen33
screen.instruct: Which of the chemicals in the puddle sample cannot be found in the control sample?
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: puddle sample
Time (minutes)
Perchloroethylene
Gasoline
Oak flooring
3-phenyltoluene
Gas chromatogram #2: control sample
